title: Can you introduce yourself?
examples: What is your name?
Where are you from?
Where do you live?
What do you do?
Do you work or study?
Do you have any children?
Do you have any pets?
What do you like to do in your free time?
Why are you studying English?
Can you say three things about yourself?
tips: Keep the first week simple and welcoming
Allow short answers and help learners expand a little
Use follow-up questions like Really? or Why?

title: What is your daily routine like?
examples: What time do you get up?
What do you do in the morning?
Do you eat breakfast every day?
What time do you start work?
Are you busy during the day?
What do you do in the afternoon?
What do you do in the evening?
What time do you go to bed?
Do you have the same routine every day?
What is your favorite part of the day?
tips: Practice simple present tense
Compare weekdays and weekends if needed
Encourage time expressions like at seven or after lunch

title: What do you eat and drink every day?
examples: What do you usually eat for breakfast?
What do you usually eat for lunch?
What do you usually eat for dinner?
Do you drink coffee or tea?
Do you cook at home?
Do you eat out often?
What food do you like?
What food do you not like?
What is your favorite drink?
Is your diet healthy?
tips: This topic works well even for shy beginners
Use pictures or examples if conversation slows down
Good chance to review like, don't like, usually, sometimes

title: What do you like to do in your free time?
examples: What do you do on weekends?
Do you have any hobbies?
Do you like watching TV?
Do you like reading books?
Do you like music?
Do you play any sports?
Do you like cooking?
Do you go out on weekends?
Do you prefer staying home or going out?
What is your favorite hobby?
tips: Let students answer with simple like + noun or like + ing forms
Ask easy follow-ups such as When? or With who?
This topic is good for building confidence

title: What is your family like?
examples: How many people are in your family?
Do you have any brothers or sisters?
Do you live with your family?
Do you have any children?
What is your mother like?
What is your father like?
Who are you close to in your family?
Do you often talk with your family?
Do you eat together?
What do you like to do with your family?
phrases: There are ... people in my family.
tips: Keep questions flexible because family situations differ
Let learners skip questions they do not want to answer
Good for practicing there is and there are, have and has

title: What is your home like?
examples: Do you live in a house or an apartment?
How many rooms do you have?
What is your favorite room?
Is your home quiet?
Do you have a big kitchen?
Do you have a desk at home?
What do you like about your home?
What do you not like about your home?
Do you clean often?
Is your room tidy now?
tips: Use familiar vocabulary and real-life description
Ask learners to describe one room in detail if needed
A good topic for there is, there are, and adjectives

title: What is your town or neighborhood like?
examples: Do you like your town?
Is your town big or small?
Is it busy or quiet?
Are there many shops near your home?
Is there a park near your home?
Do you have a favorite place in your town?
Do you go there often?
Is your town convenient?
How do you usually get around?
What is good about your neighborhood?
phrases: There is a ... near my home.
tips: This topic connects well to place vocabulary
Use maps or nearby examples if helpful
Review there is, there are, and location words

title: How do you usually go places?
examples: How do you go to work or school?
Do you usually walk?
Do you take the train?
Do you drive a car?
Do you ride a bicycle?
How long does it take to get there?
What transportation do you like best?
What transportation do you not like?
Is public transportation good in your area?
Do you like traveling by train?
tips: Great for transportation vocabulary
Practice by + train, by + bus, on foot
Ask about daily habits first, not abstract opinions

title: What do you do when you go shopping?
examples: Do you like shopping?
Where do you usually shop?
Do you shop online?
What do you often buy?
Do you like supermarkets?
Do you go to convenience stores often?
Do you make a shopping list?
Do you enjoy window shopping?
What did you buy recently?
Do you like shopping alone or with someone?
tips: Easy topic because everyone has some shopping routine
Good for daily vocabulary and recent past practice
Convenience stores are a nice subtopic in Japan

title: What kind of weather do you like?
examples: What is the weather like today?
Do you like sunny days?
Do you like rainy days?
Do you like hot weather?
Do you like cold weather?
What season do you like best?
What do you do on rainy days?
Do you check the weather forecast?
How is the weather in your town?
What season do you not like?
tips: Very safe and easy topic for beginners
Use it as warm-up if needed
Good for adjectives like hot, cold, sunny, windy

title: What do you like about spring?
examples: Do you like spring?
What is nice about spring?
Do you see flowers near your home?
Is spring busy for you?
Do you go out more in spring?
What clothes do you wear in spring?
Do you have allergies in spring?
What food do you like in spring?
Do you enjoy cherry blossom season?
What do you usually do in spring?
phrases: One thing I like about spring is ...
tips: Seasonal topics are good because they repeat naturally each year
Keep answers personal and concrete
Use simple because sentences

title: What do you do in summer?
examples: Do you like summer?
Is summer too hot for you?
What do you eat in summer?
What do you drink in summer?
Do you travel in summer?
Do you go to the beach or pool?
Do you like festivals?
Do you stay indoors a lot in summer?
What clothes do you wear in summer?
What do you enjoy in summer?
tips: This topic brings out familiar seasonal vocabulary
Use food, events, and weather to keep it easy
Helpful for talking about habits and preferences

title: What do you enjoy in autumn?
examples: Do you like autumn?
What food do you like in autumn?
Do you enjoy cooler weather?
Do you like autumn leaves?
Do you go anywhere in autumn?
What clothes do you wear in autumn?
Do you like walking in autumn?
What do you drink in autumn?
Do you feel more relaxed in autumn?
What is your favorite thing about autumn?
tips: Autumn is easy to talk about through food and weather
Encourage sensory words like cool, warm, beautiful
Let learners compare summer and autumn

title: How do you spend winter?
examples: Do you like winter?
What do you do on cold days?
Do you drink hot drinks in winter?
What food do you like in winter?
Do you wear coats or sweaters often?
Do you like snow?
Do you stay home more in winter?
Do you travel in winter?
What is hard about winter?
What do you like about winter?
tips: Easy topic with lots of daily-life vocabulary
Good for likes and dislikes
Use simple contrast like I like ... but I don't like ...

title: What do you do on weekends?
examples: What do you usually do on Saturdays?
What do you usually do on Sundays?
Do you stay home on weekends?
Do you go out on weekends?
Do you meet your friends or family?
Do you study English on weekends?
Do you sleep in on weekends?
What did you do last weekend?
What will you do this weekend?
What is your ideal weekend?
tips: A very useful topic because learners can answer from real life
Great for mixing present, past, and future at a basic level
Ideal for regular weekly conversation practice

title: What do you do with your phone or computer?
examples: Do you use your phone a lot?
What apps do you use every day?
Do you watch videos on your phone?
Do you use social media?
Do you use your phone for work?
Do you use your phone for study?
Do you use a computer every day?
What do you usually do online?
Do you shop online?
What app do you like best?
tips: Modern and easy topic for adults
Keep vocabulary practical and familiar
Good chance to practice for + noun and to + verb meanings

title: What do you watch or listen to?
examples: Do you watch TV?
Do you watch YouTube?
What kind of videos do you like?
Do you like movies?
Do you like dramas?
Do you listen to music every day?
What kind of music do you like?
Do you listen to podcasts?
Do you watch anything in English?
What did you watch recently?
tips: Very useful because learners often enjoy sharing preferences
Good topic for simple genre vocabulary
Can connect naturally to English learning habits

title: Do you like cooking and eating at home?
examples: Do you cook every day?
What do you usually cook?
Is cooking easy for you?
Do you cook for yourself or your family?
What is easy for you to make?
What is difficult for you to make?
Do you like trying new recipes?
Who cooks in your home?
What did you cook recently?
What do you want to cook someday?
phrases: It is easy for me to make ...
tips: A strong topic for home life and daily vocabulary
Use simple recipe or ingredient words if needed
Follow up with When? How often? and For who?

title: Where would you like to go?
examples: Do you like traveling?
Where did you go recently?
Do you want to travel this year?
What place do you want to visit?
Do you prefer cities or nature?
Do you like hotels?
Do you like short trips?
Do you travel with family or friends?
What do you usually do on trips?
What is your dream destination?
tips: Keep it simple and personal, not too abstract
Use want to and like to for easy output
Good topic for motivation and imagination

title: How do you study English?
examples: Why do you study English?
When do you study English?
How often do you study English?
Do you study every day?
Do you like speaking practice?
Do you like listening practice?
Do you use apps to study English?
Do you watch videos in English?
What is difficult for you in English?
What do you want to improve?
tips: Very relevant for Epura users and builds connection
Keep questions supportive and encouraging
Useful for goal language like want to and need to

title: What is your work or study life like?
examples: Do you work or study?
What do you do?
Are you busy these days?
What time do you start?
Do you work at home or outside?
What do you like about your work or study?
What is difficult about it?
Do you talk with many people?
Do you use English at work or school?
What do you do after work or school?
phrases: I like ... about my work or study.
tips: Keep questions broad so many learners can answer
Do not make it too professional or detailed
A good topic for daily-life conversation, not job interviews

title: How do you stay healthy?
examples: Do you exercise?
What exercise do you do?
Do you walk every day?
Do you sleep well?
Do you eat healthy food?
Do you drink a lot of water?
How do you relax?
Do you stretch at home?
What do you do when you are tired?
What healthy habit do you have?
tips: Keep it light and everyday, not medical
Good for healthy habits and routine language
Use try to as a helpful beginner phrase

title: What do you do on rainy days?
examples: Do you like rainy days?
Do rainy days make you sleepy?
Do you stay home on rainy days?
What do you usually do at home on rainy days?
Do you read or watch movies?
Do you work better on rainy days?
Do you go out when it rains?
What do you eat or drink on rainy days?
What is difficult on rainy days?
What do you like about rainy days?
tips: Simple but surprisingly easy to expand
Good for cozy daily-life talk
This topic works well for both quiet and chatty learners

title: What are your favorite indoor activities?
examples: Do you like staying home?
What do you do at home for fun?
Do you watch movies at home?
Do you read books at home?
Do you cook or bake at home?
Do you do crafts or hobbies indoors?
Do you clean or organize at home?
Do you exercise at home?
What is your favorite thing to do indoors?
What is a perfect day at home for you?
phrases: A perfect day at home is ...
tips: Good topic for relaxed conversation
Easy for learners who do not travel or go out much
Use home vocabulary and hobby vocabulary together

title: What are your plans for the near future?
examples: What will you do this weekend?
What will you do tonight?
Do you have any plans for next week?
Do you want to go anywhere soon?
Do you want to try anything new?
Do you have any family plans?
Do you have any study plans?
Do you have any travel plans?
What are you looking forward to?
What do you want to do soon?
tips: A nice way to practice simple future expressions
Use near future, not big life goals
Perfect for keeping the conversation practical and easy

title: What have you enjoyed recently?
examples: What made you happy recently?
What did you enjoy this week?
Did you eat anything good recently?
Did you go anywhere nice recently?
Did you watch anything interesting recently?
Did you buy anything nice recently?
Did you talk with anyone special recently?
Did you try anything new recently?
What was one good thing in your life recently?
What do you want to enjoy next?
tips: This is a friendly wrap-up week before looping back
Focus on small happy moments, not deep reflection
A nice topic for building positive community energy
